title: Lesson 5: Adjective Agreement — Gender, Number & Case
description: One of Pashto's most morphologically complex systems: adjectives inflect for the gender, number, and case of the noun they modify. Five adjective classes plus irregular forms are introduced, covering the full 4-cell agreement grid (sg.m / sg.f / pl.m / pl.f) in both direct and oblique case. Introduces the oblique case for the first time in the context of adjective phrases — a preview of the ergative system appearing in Lesson 13. Communicative context: weather, seasons, Afghan New Year (Nawruz), and describing people and places. Pashto script primary throughout.

content.heading: Communicative Objectives
lines: By the end of this lesson you will be able to: describe people, places, and objects using adjectives that agree in gender, number, and case with the noun they modify.
You will produce the correct inflected form of Class 1 through Class 5 adjectives — and selected irregular adjectives — in attributive (pre-nominal) and predicative (post-verbal) positions.
You will use the oblique case form of adjectives correctly inside prepositional phrases, building on the پهـ...کې [pa...ke] structure from Lesson 2.

You will apply a core set of colour and descriptive adjectives — سپين [spin] 'white', تور [tor] 'black', سور [sur] 'red', زرد [zard] 'yellow', شين [shin] 'green/blue', اوچت [ucat] 'tall', کوچنی [kucnay] 'small', نوی [naway] 'new' — in gendered, cased agreement.
You will ask and answer vocabulary questions using the classroom phrase پهـ پښتو کې ... ته څه وايي؟ [pa paxto ke ... ta tse wāyi?] 'What is the word for ... in Pashto?'

content.heading: Section 1 — Dialogue: هوا او موسمونه (Weather and Seasons)
lines: Amēn and Joan are talking about the weather in Kabul versus Joan's hometown. This dialogue introduces adjectives describing weather and seasons — the gateway to the full agreement system.
ژون: امین، هوا ډيره سړه ده! [jon: amēn, hawā dera sara da!] — Joan: Amēn, the weather is very cold!
امين: هو، ژمی ډير سوړ وي. [amēn: haw, žəmay der soṛ wi.] — Amēn: Yes, winter is usually very cold.
ژون: معمولاً داسې سوړ وي؟ [jon: māmolan dāse soṛ wi?] — Joan: Is it usually this cold?
امين: هو. کله کله داسې ډيرې واوره ورې چې لارې بندې شي. [amēn: haw. kāla kāla dāse dere wāwre wori če lāre bande shi.] — Amēn: Yes. Sometimes so much snow falls that the roads get blocked.
ژون: پهـ ستاسو ښار کې اوړی ډير تود وي؟ [jon: pa stāso xār ke oṛay der tod wi?] — Joan: Is summer very hot in your city?
امين: نه، اوړی نارم وي، خو ژمی سخت سوړ وي. [amēn: na, oṛay nāram wi, xo žəmay saxt soṛ wi.] — Amēn: No, summer is pleasant, but winter is very cold.
ژون: زما ښار کې اوړی ډير تود وي. [jon: zmā xār ke oṛay der tod wi.] — Joan: In my city summer is very hot.
امين: نن ورځ هوا ښه ده. [amēn: nan wradz hawā xa da.] — Amēn: Today the weather is good.
ژون: هو! ډير ښه ده. له هوا پرته ژوند نه کيږي! [jon: haw! der xa da. la hawā prata žwand na kegi!] — Joan: Yes! Very good. You can't get along without weather!
GRAMMAR SPOTLIGHT on the dialogue — find the agreement:
هوا ډيره سړه ده [hawā dera sara da] — هوا [hawā] 'weather' is F3; therefore ډيره [dera] and سړه [sara] both take FEMININE forms → ډير [der] M becomes ډيره [dera] F ✓
ژمی ډير سوړ وي [žəmay der soṛ wi] — ژمی [žəmay] 'winter' is M3; therefore ډير [der] and سوړ [soṛ] stay in MASCULINE form ✓
لارې بندې شي [lāre bande shi] — لارې [lāre] 'roads' is F1 plural; therefore بندې [bande] takes FEMININE PLURAL form ✓

content.heading: 🎯 The Core Rule: Adjectives Chase the Noun
lines: Every Pashto adjective must match the noun it describes in THREE dimensions simultaneously: (1) GENDER — masculine or feminine, (2) NUMBER — singular or plural, (3) CASE — direct or oblique.
This means a single adjective like نوی [naway] 'new' has up to EIGHT different surface forms depending on what noun it modifies.
Think of the adjective as a shadow that mirrors its noun exactly. Whatever form the noun takes, the adjective takes a parallel form from its own class paradigm.
THE TWO POSITIONS: Adjectives appear in two positions in Pashto sentences —
ATTRIBUTIVE (before the noun): نوی شاګرد [naway shāgerd] — 'a new student' → adjective precedes and agrees with the noun.
PREDICATIVE (after the copula): شاګرد نوی دی [shāgerd naway da] — 'The student is new' → adjective follows the verb and still agrees with the subject noun.

OBLIQUE POSITION (inside a prepositional phrase): پهـ نوي کور کې [pa nawi kor ke] — 'in the new house' → the noun کور [kor] is in oblique case (object of پهـ...کې), so the adjective نوی [naway] → نوي [nawi] also takes oblique form.
This oblique agreement is a critical preview of the full case system you will encounter in Lesson 13.

content.heading: Section 2 — The Five Adjective Classes
lines: Pashto adjectives fall into five regular classes plus irregular forms. The class determines what endings the adjective takes for each gender/number/case combination. Master these five paradigms and you can inflect the vast majority of Pashto adjectives.
CLASS 1 — The most common class. Masculine forms are the same in all positions EXCEPT oblique plural. Feminine forms follow F1 noun endings.
Example: جوړ [joṛ] 'well / healthy'
M DSg: جوړ [joṛ] ◆ M DPl: جوړ [joṛ] ◆ M OSg: جوړ [joṛ] ◆ M OPl: جوړو [joṛo]
F DSg: جوړه [joṛa] ◆ F DPl: جوړې [joṛe] ◆ F OSg: جوړې [joṛe] ◆ F OPl: جوړو [joṛo]

Class 1 adjectives: جوړ [joṛ] 'well/healthy' ◆ سوړ [soṛ] 'cold' ◆ سپين [spin] 'white' ◆ تور [tor] 'black' ◆ سور [sur] 'red' ◆ زرد [zard] 'yellow' ◆ شين [shin] 'green/blue' ◆ اوچت [ucat] 'tall' ◆ زوړ [zoṛ] 'old (thing)' ◆ لوڅ [luts] 'bare/naked' ◆ ناروغ [nārogh] 'sick/ill' ◆ کلن [kalan] 'yearly/annual' ◆ ښه [xa] 'good' (irregular spelling but Class 1 endings)
CLASS 2 — Masculine forms follow M3 noun endings (-ای → -ي → -ي → -و). Feminine forms follow F1 endings.
Example: نوی [naway] 'new'
M DSg: نوی [naway] ◆ M DPl: نوي [nawi] ◆ M OSg: نوي [nawi] ◆ M OPl: نوو [nawo]
F DSg: نوې [nawe] ◆ F DPl: نوې [nawe] ◆ F OSg: نوې [nawe] ◆ F OPl: نوو [nawo]

Class 2 adjectives: نوی [naway] 'new' ◆ کوچنی [kucnay] 'small/little' ◆ ليونی [lewanay] 'crazy' ◆ وږی [wegay] 'hungry' ◆ تږی [tegay] 'thirsty' ◆ خوبناک [xobāwsay] 'sleepy' ◆ خپه [xapa] 'sad' (Class 4)

content.heading: Adjective Classes 3, 4, and 5
lines: CLASS 3 — Masculine DSg ends in [-a]. All masculine forms except OPl are the same. Feminine follows F1.
Example: ښه [xa] 'good' (Class 3 pattern — note: all M forms use [-a] or bare form)
M DSg: ښه [xa] ◆ M DPl: ښه [xa] ◆ M OSg: ښه [xa] ◆ M OPl: ښو [xo]
F DSg: ښه [xa] ◆ F DPl: ښې [xe] ◆ F OSg: ښې [xe] ◆ F OPl: ښو [xo]
Class 3 adjectives: ښه [xa] 'good' ◆ بد [bad] 'bad' ◆ خراب [xarāb] 'bad/broken' ◆ مشهور [mashhur] 'famous' — note these use [-a] for feminine DSg
CLASS 4 — ALL FORMS ARE IDENTICAL — the most learner-friendly class. The adjective never changes regardless of gender, number, or case.
Example: ښايسته [xāysta] 'beautiful/good-looking'
ALL FORMS: ښايسته [xāysta] — the same in every position.

Class 4 adjectives: ښايسته [xāysta] 'beautiful' ◆ منتظر [muntazir] 'waiting' ◆ ماریض [māriz] 'sick' ◆ خاص [xās] 'special' ◆ مصروف [masruf] 'busy' ◆ ننګيالی [nangayālay] 'brave' — borrowings from Arabic/Persian typically fall here
CLASS 5 — Masculine DSg ends in [-or] or [-ar]. The [-o-] or [-a-] vowel shifts: masculine oblique and all feminine forms replace it with [-a-].
Example: سوړ [soṛ] 'cold'
M DSg: سوړ [soṛ] ◆ M DPl: سوړه [soṛa] ◆ M OSg: سوړ [soṛ] ◆ M OPl: سوړه [soṛa]
F DSg: سړه [sara] ◆ F DPl: سړه [sara] ◆ F OSg: سړه [sara] ◆ F OPl: سړو [saro]
Class 5 adjectives: سوړ/سړ [soṛ/sar] 'cold' ◆ توده/تود [toda/tod] 'hot/warm' (irregular — see below)

content.heading: Irregular Adjectives — Memorise These Paradigms
lines: Several high-frequency adjectives do not fit neatly into any class. They must be memorised as complete paradigms.
تود [tod] 'HOT / WARM' (irregular — [o] → [aw] alternation):
M DSg: تود [tod] ◆ M DPl/OSg: تاوده [tāwda] ◆ M OPl: تاوده [tāwda]
F DSg: تاوده [tāwda] ◆ F DPl/OSg: تاوده [tāwda] ◆ F OPl: تاودو [tāwdo]
نارم [nāram] 'PLEASANT / MILD' (irregular — stem change in feminine):
M DSg/DPl/OSg: نارم [nāram] ◆ M OPl: نارمو [nāramo]
F DSg: نارمه [nārama] ◆ F DPl/OSg: نارمې [nārame] ◆ F OPl: نارمو [nāramo]
خوږ [xwag] 'SWEET' (irregular — [ā] vowel in oblique forms):
M DSg: خوږ [xwag] ◆ M DPl/OSg: خواږه [xwāga] ◆ M OPl: خواږو [xwāgo]
F DSg: خوږه [xwaga] ◆ F DPl/OSg: خوږې [xwage] ◆ F OPl: خوږو [xwago]
لمده [lamda] 'WET' (irregular — feminine base form differs):
M DSg: لمد [lamd] ◆ M DPl/OSg: لمده [lamda] ◆ M OPl: لمدو [lamdo]
F DSg: لمده [lamda] ◆ F DPl/OSg: لمدې [lamde] ◆ F OPl: لمدو [lamdo]
ستر [ster] 'BIG / GREAT' (suppletive — plural uses a completely different stem):
M DSg: ستر [ster] ◆ M DPl: سترن [stern] (← DIFFERENT STEM!) ◆ M OSg: ستر [ster] ◆ M OPl: سترنو [sterno]
F DSg: سترې [stere] ◆ F DPl: سترنې [sterne] ◆ F OSg: سترې [stere] ◆ F OPl: سترنو [sterno]

MEMORY NOTE on ستر [ster]: The suppletive plural سترن [stern] has no relation to the singular ستر [ster] — they come from different historical roots. This is one of the truly unpredictable forms in Pashto morphology. Memorise both forms together as a pair: ستر / سترن [ster / stern].

content.heading: ⚙️ The 4-Cell Agreement Grid — Your Reference Framework
lines: Every Pashto adjective inflects across a 4-cell DIRECT grid and a 4-cell OBLIQUE grid. For practical use, memorise the four DIRECT forms first (these appear in attributive and predicative positions), then learn the oblique forms (needed inside prepositional phrases).
THE 4 DIRECT FORMS — which form of the adjective do you use?
→ Modifying a MASCULINE SINGULAR noun (direct case): use M DSg form. Example: نوی کور [naway kor] 'a new house'
→ Modifying a MASCULINE PLURAL noun (direct case): use M DPl form. Example: نوي کورونه [nawi korunā] 'new houses'
→ Modifying a FEMININE SINGULAR noun (direct case): use F DSg form. Example: نوې ليلیه [nawe layliyā] 'a new dormitory'
→ Modifying a FEMININE PLURAL noun (direct case): use F DPl form. Example: نوې ليلیې [nawe layliye] 'new dormitories'
THE OBLIQUE SHIFT — when the noun is inside a prepositional phrase:
→ پهـ نوي کور کې [pa nawi kor ke] — 'in the new house' — کور [kor] (M OSg) triggers نوي [nawi] (M OSg of نوی)
→ پهـ نوي ليلیه کې [pa nawe layliyā ke] — 'in the new dorm' — ليلیه [layliyā] (F OSg) triggers نوې [nawe] (F OSg of نوی)

SHORTCUT: For Class 2 adjectives like نوی [naway], the M OSg and F DSg/DPl/OSg forms are all -ې [e] or -ي [i] endings. For Class 1 adjectives like جوړ [joṛ], the masculine forms do NOT change at all (except OPl). So the masculine oblique of Class 1 = the masculine direct!

content.heading: Section 3 — Colour Vocabulary & Agreement Drill (رنګونه)
lines: Colours are among the most frequently used adjectives — and all follow Class 1 (except شين [shin] which also follows Class 1). Master colours first to build agreement fluency.
سپين [spin] 'WHITE' — Class 1:
M DSg: سپين [spin] ◆ F DSg: سپينه [spina] ◆ M/F DPl: سپين / سپينې [spin / spine] ◆ F OSg: سپينې [spine]
تور [tor] 'BLACK' — Class 1:
M DSg: تور [tor] ◆ F DSg: توره [tora] ◆ F DPl/OSg: تورې [tore]
سور [sur] 'RED' — Class 1:
M DSg: سور [sur] ◆ F DSg: سوره [sura] ◆ F DPl/OSg: سورې [sure]
زرد [zard] 'YELLOW' — Class 1:
M DSg: زرد [zard] ◆ F DSg: زرده [zarda] ◆ F DPl/OSg: زردې [zarde]
شين [shin] 'GREEN / BLUE' — Class 1 (one word covers both green and blue!):
M DSg: شين [shin] ◆ F DSg: شنه [shna] ◆ F DPl/OSg: شنې [shne]
ATTRIBUTION DRILLS (Script only — check your agreement):
سپين کور — 'white house' (M DSg) ◆ سپينه ليلیه — 'white dormitory' (F DSg)
توره شپه — 'black night' (F DSg) ◆ سور کتاب — 'red book' (M DSg)
شين چای — 'green tea' (M DSg) ◆ شنه مزدکه — 'green soil/field' (F DSg)
PREDICATION DRILLS (adjective after copula — still agrees with subject):
کور سپين دی. — 'The house is white.' (M DSg subject → M DSg adjective سپين)
ليلیه سپينه ده. — 'The dormitory is white.' (F DSg subject → F DSg adjective سپينه)
کورونه سپين دي. — 'The houses are white.' (M DPl subject → M DPl adjective سپين)
OBLIQUE DRILLS (inside prepositional phrase):
پهـ سپين کور کې — 'in the white house' (M OSg: سپين stays same — Class 1!)
پهـ سپينې ليلیه کې — 'in the white dormitory' (F OSg: سپينه → سپينې)

content.heading: 🌈 Cultural Insight: Colour Symbolism in Pashtun Culture
lines: Colours carry deep symbolic weight in Pashtun culture — knowing these associations will help you understand poetry, conversation, and traditional settings far beyond their literal meanings.

سپين [spin] 'WHITE' — symbolises PURITY, PEACE, and GOODNESS. سپين زړه [spin zṛa] 'white heart' means 'pure heart / sincere person'. The white flag (سپينه بيرغه [spina bayraga]) traditionally signals truce or surrender.

تور [tor] 'BLACK' — symbolises MOURNING, GRIEF, and ENMITY. توره ورځ [tora wradz] 'black day' means 'terrible day'. تور مخ [tor makh] 'black face' is a serious insult meaning 'shameful person'. Use with caution.

شين [shin] 'GREEN / BLUE' — ISLAMIC SIGNIFICANCE. Green is the colour of Islam and Paradise; it appears on the Afghan flag. شين [shin] covers both green and blue in Pashto — context distinguishes them, or more specific terms are used.
سور [sur] 'RED' — associated with BLOOD, LIFE, and CELEBRATION. Red is a wedding colour in many Pashtun traditions.
زرد [zard] 'YELLOW' — can carry negative connotations of ILLNESS or COWARDICE in certain idiomatic expressions, though it is also simply 'yellow' as a neutral descriptor.

IMPORTANT: Physical descriptions of people must follow gender norms. Commenting on a person's appearance is socially normal in Pashtun contexts, but male speakers should exercise care when describing women in mixed company. The adjective ښايسته [xāysta] 'beautiful' (Class 4 — invariable) is the most neutral descriptor.

content.heading: Section 4 — New Vocabulary (نوي ټکي)
lines: SEASONS (all M3 class — direct singular ends in -ی [-ay]):
بهار / پسرلی [pasarlay] — spring (M3 class) [pasarlay]
اوړی [oṛay] — summer (M3 class) [oṛay]
منی [manay] — autumn / fall (M3 class) [manay]
ژمی [žəmay] — winter (M3 class) [žəmay]
WEATHER NOUNS:
هوا [hawā] — weather / air (F3 class) [hawā]
واوره [wāwra] — snow (F3 class) [wāwra]
باران [bārān] — rain (M2 class) [bārān]
باد [bād] — wind (M2 class) [bād]
CALENDAR / TIME NOUNS:
کال [kāl] — year (M2 class) [kāl]
موسم [mosam] — season (M2 class) [mosam]
نوروز [nawroz] — New Year (Afghan Solar New Year, March 21) (M2 class) [nawroz]
ورځ [wradz] — day (F1 irregular) [wradz]
مياشت [myāsht] — month (F1 irregular) [myāsht]
رخصتي [rukhsati] — holiday (F1 irregular) [rukhsati]
NATURE NOUNS:
ګل [gul] — flower (M1 class) [gul]
فصل [fasal] — crop / harvest (M2 class) [fasal]
هيواد [hiwād] — country / homeland (M2 class) [hiwād]
ADJECTIVES (new this lesson):
سخت [saxt] — heavy / hard / very (Class 1 or intensifier) [saxt]
خوږ [xwag] — sweet (irregular — see paradigm above) [xwag]
نور [nor] — other (Class 1) [nor]
ارام [ārām] — public / open / calm (Class 1) [ārām]
ټيک [ṭik] — some / a certain (Class 4) [ṭik]
هر [har] — every / each [har]
حتی [hata] — even [hata]
VERBS (new this lesson):
ورېدل [woreḍəl] — to fall (snow or rain); stem: وره- [wore-] [woreḍəl]
بنديدل [bandeḍəl] — to become blocked; stem: بنده- [bande-] [bandeḍəl]
پخيدل [paxeḍəl] — to ripen; stem: پخه- [paxe-] [paxeḍəl]
ADVERBS & PHRASES:

معمولاً [māmulan] — usually [māmulan] ◆ ډير [der] — very (also: many) ◆ نن [nan] — today [nan] ◆ اکثراً [aksaran] — often [aksaran] ◆ دومره [domra] — so much / so many [domra] ◆ له...پرته [la...prata] — without ◆ X ته Y وايي [X ta Y wāyi] — X is called Y (lit. 'they say Y for X')
USEFUL CLASSROOM PHRASE — Asking for vocabulary:
پهـ پښتو کې سلام ته څه وايي؟ [pa paxto ke salām ta tse wāyi?] — 'What do they say for 'hello' in Pashto?' (lit. 'In Pashto, what do they say for 'hello'?')
دا ته پښتو کې څه وايي؟ [da ta paxto ke tse wāyi?] — 'What is the Pashto word for this?' (pointing to nearby object)
هغه شی څه دی؟ [hagha shay tse da?] — 'What is that thing?' (pointing to distant object)

content.heading: Section 5 — Attributive vs. Predicative vs. Oblique: The Three Positions
lines: The agreement rule is the same in all three positions — but the sentences look different. Master all three to produce and recognise adjective agreement correctly.
POSITION 1 — ATTRIBUTIVE (adjective PRECEDES the noun it modifies, inside a noun phrase):
نوی شاګرد [naway shāgerd] — 'a new student' (M DSg: نوی agrees with شاګرد M DSg)
نوې شاګرده [nawe shāgərda] — 'a new (female) student' (F DSg: نوې agrees with شاګرده F DSg)
نوي شاګردان [nawi shāgerdān] — 'new students (M)' (M DPl: نوي agrees with شاګردان M DPl)
نوې شاګردې [nawe shāgərde] — 'new students (F)' (F DPl: نوې agrees with شاګردې F DPl)
POSITION 2 — PREDICATIVE (adjective follows the copula — it describes the SUBJECT, which may be distant in the sentence):
شاګرد نوی دی. [shāgerd naway da.] — 'The student is new.' (Subject شاګرد is M DSg → adjective نوی M DSg)
شاګرده نوې ده. [shāgərda nawe da.] — 'The (female) student is new.' (Subject شاګرده is F DSg → adjective نوې F DSg)
هوا ډيره سړه ده. [hawā dera sara da.] — 'The weather is very cold.' (هوا is F → سړه is F DSg of Class 5 سوړ [soṛ])
POSITION 3 — OBLIQUE inside پهـ...کې [pa...ke] (adjective agrees with noun in OBLIQUE case):
پهـ نوي کور کې [pa nawi kor ke] — 'in the new house' (کور M OSg → نوی → نوي M OSg)
پهـ نوې ليلیه کې [pa nawe layliyā ke] — 'in the new dorm' (ليلیه F OSg → نوی → نوې F OSg)
پهـ سپين کور کې [pa spin kor ke] — 'in the white house' (Class 1: سپين M OSg = same as DSg — NO change!)
پهـ سپينې ليلیه کې [pa spine layliyā ke] — 'in the white dorm' (Class 1 F: سپينه DSg → سپينې OSg — vowel shortens)
KEY CONTRAST — Class 1 vs. Class 2 in oblique:
Class 1 masculine OSg = same as DSg: سپين کور (direct) → پهـ سپين کور کې (oblique) ← NO CHANGE
Class 2 masculine OSg changes: نوی کور (direct) → پهـ نوي کور کې (oblique) ← -ای → -ي

content.heading: 💡 Agreement Shortcut: The Feminine Suffix Map
lines: For Class 1 adjectives (the largest and most common class), the feminine forms follow a highly predictable pattern — almost identical to F1 noun endings:
F DSg: add -ه [a] to the masculine base → سپين + ه = سپينه [spina]
F DPl: change -ه [a] to -ې [e] → سپينه → سپينې [spine]
F OSg: same as F DPl → سپينې [spine]
F OPl: change -ه [a] to -و [o] → سپينو [spino]

PHONOLOGICAL ALERT: The vowel distinction between -ه [a] (short) and -ا [ā] (long) in agreement suffixes is PHONEMICALLY CONTRASTIVE. سپينه [spina] (F DSg adjective) vs. سپينا [spinā] (would indicate a different word entirely). Train your ear to hear this length distinction on audio — it is not merely orthographic.
For Class 2 adjectives, the feminine forms use -ې [e] throughout the direct case (DSg, DPl, OSg all use -ې [e]). Only OPl uses -و [o]: نوې [nawe] (F DSg/DPl/OSg) → نوو [nawo] (F OPl).
MEMORY TRICK: 'Feminine adjectives like e's and o's' — the F forms consistently use -ه/-ې/-و endings. The masculine Class 1 forms mostly look the same as the bare stem.

content.heading: Section 6 — Reading: د افغانستان موسمونه (The Seasons of Afghanistan)
lines: This reading is in Pashto script only. Topic: Afghan seasons and the Nawruz New Year celebration. The passage is rich in adjective usage — identify each adjective and confirm its agreement with the noun it modifies.
افغانستان يو ښکلی هيواد دی. هر موسم يې بيل رنګ لري.
بهار ډير ښه موسم دی. هوا نارمه وي. ګلونه پرانيزي. د افغانستان خلک نوروز ولمانځي.
نوروز د بهار لومړۍ ورځ ده. هغه ورځ ډيره مهمه ده.
اوړی ډير تود وي. ګرمي ډيره سختي وي. ميوه پخيږي.
منی ډير ښه وي. هوا نارمه او خوږه وي. فصلونه ډير ښه دي.
ژمی سخت سوړ وي. واوره ډيره ورېږي. لارې بندې شي.
READING GLOSSARY:

ښکلی [xkəlay] — beautiful (Class 2 adjective, M DSg) ◆ بيل [bel] — separate / different (Class 1) ◆ رنګ [rang] — colour (M2 class) ◆ پرانيزي [pranizi] — (they/flowers) open (3pl present imperfective) ◆ ولمانځي [wolmāndzi] — celebrate (3pl present perfective) ◆ لومړۍ [lomṛay] — first (ordinal, F DSg because agrees with ورځ which is F) ◆ مهمه [mahama] — important (Class 1, F DSg) ◆ ګرمي [garmi] — heat (F1 class) ◆ سختي [saxti] — hardship/intensity ◆ ميوه پخيږي [mewa paxegi] — fruit ripens (3sg F present imperfective)
COMPREHENSION QUESTIONS — answer in Pashto script:
① د افغانستان بهار ددی دی؟ — What is Afghan spring like?
② نوروز د کوم موسم لومړۍ ورځ ده؟ — Nawruz is the first day of which season?
③ پهـ ژمي کې هوا ددی وي؟ — What is the weather like in winter?

content.heading: 🌸 Cultural Spotlight: نوروز — Afghan New Year
lines: نوروز [nawroz] — literally 'New Day' — is the Afghan Solar New Year, celebrated on March 21 (the spring equinox). It is the most significant national holiday in Afghanistan, pre-dating Islam by thousands of years and rooted in Zoroastrian / ancient Iranian tradition.

Nawruz marks the first day of the Solar Hijri calendar month of حمل [hamal] (Aries), which you will study fully in Lesson 14. The holiday is observed across Afghanistan, Iran, Central Asia, and the broader Persian cultural sphere.

CELEBRATIONS: Traditional Nawruz practices include بهاري سیر [bahāri ser] (spring picnics/excursions), بوزکشي [buzkashi] (the traditional Afghan horse sport), and family gatherings. In Kabul, the Nawruz flag-raising at Mazar-e-Sharif's Blue Mosque is a nationally televised event.

GRAMMAR CONNECTION: د بهار لومړۍ ورځ [da bahār lomṛay wradz] — 'the first day of spring' — the ordinal adjective لومړۍ [lomṛay] is feminine because ورځ [wradz] 'day' is a feminine irregular noun. This is adjective agreement in action: even ordinal numbers must agree with the noun they modify.
NOTE: Nawruz is celebrated by all ethnic groups in Afghanistan — Pashtun, Tajik, Hazara, Uzbek — making it a genuinely national cultural touchstone rather than an ethnic-specific holiday.

content.heading: Section 7 — Asking for Vocabulary (د ټکو غوښتل)
lines: This section gives you essential classroom survival phrases for expanding your Pashto vocabulary organically through conversation.
TO ASK WHAT SOMETHING IS CALLED IN PASHTO:
پهـ پښتو کې [word/concept] ته څه وايي؟ [pa paxto ke [word] ta tse wāyi?] — 'What do they say in Pashto for [word]?'
Example: پهـ پښتو کې 'سلام' ته څه وايي؟ — 'What do they say in Pashto for 'hello'?'
Answer pattern: [پښتو word] وايي. [... wāyi.] — 'They say [...].'  or  دا ته ... وايي. [da ta ... wāyi.] — 'For this they say ...'
TO POINT AT A NEARBY OBJECT AND ASK:
دا ته پښتو کې څه وايي؟ [da ta paxto ke tse wāyi?] — 'What is the Pashto word for this?' (proximal دا [da] = this, nearby)
TO POINT AT A DISTANT OBJECT AND ASK:
هغه شی څه دی؟ [hagha shay tse da?] — 'What is that thing?' (distal هغه [hagha] = that, far away)
TO ASK THE MEANING OF A PASHTO WORD:
دا ... تسه ماناؤ؟ [da ... tse māna?] — 'What does ... mean?'
Answer: دا تسه... [da tse ...] — 'It means...'
EXAMPLE: دا 'نوروز' تسه ماناؤ؟ — 'What does 'Nawroz' mean?' → د بهار لومړۍ ورځ ته نوروز وايي. — 'The first day of spring is called Nawroz.'

GRAMMAR NOTE: The verb وايي [wāyi] 'they say' is the 3rd person plural present imperfective of ويل [wayəl] 'to say'. In this vocabulary-asking construction, the impersonal 3rd plural is used — equivalent to the English impersonal 'one says' or 'they call it'. This construction will become increasingly important as you encounter more authentic Pashto.

content.heading: Section 8 — Diversion: پښتو شعر — Mountain Couplet (د غرونو شعر)
lines: پهـ لوړو غرو د خدای نظر دی.
پهـ سر يې واوره وروي، پهـ لمن کې ګلونه.
[pa loṛo gharo da xudāy nazar da.
pa sar ye wāwra worawi, pa lamən ke guluna.]
Translation: 'The high mountains are under God's watchful care. At their summit He causes snow to fall; at their foot, flowers.'
WORD-FOR-WORD GLOSS:

پهـ لوړو غرو [pa loṛo gharo] — 'in/on the high mountains' (oblique phrase!) ◆ لوړو [loṛo] — high (Class 1, M OPl — agreeing with غرو [gharo] M OPl) ◆ غرو [gharo] — mountains (M OPl oblique plural of غر [ghar]) ◆ د خدای [da xudāy] — of God ◆ نظر [nazar] — gaze / watchful care / blessing (M2) ◆ پهـ سر يې [pa sar ye] — 'at their summit' (يې = weak possessive clitic) ◆ واوره وروي [wāwra worawi] — causes snow to fall ◆ پهـ لمن کې [pa lamən ke] — 'at the foot (of the mountain)' ◆ لمن [lamən] — skirt/foot (of mountain) ◆ ګلونه [gulune] — flowers (M2 DPl)

ADJECTIVE GRAMMAR IN THE POEM: پهـ لوړو غرو — the oblique plural phrase is a perfect showcase of agreement in action. لوړ [loṛ] 'high' is a Class 1 adjective. The noun غر [ghar] 'mountain' is masculine. In the oblique plural: غرو [gharo] (M OPl) → لوړو [loṛo] (Class 1 M OPl — the -و [-o] ending, the only changed form for masculine Class 1). This single phrase demonstrates the full oblique agreement system.

CULTURAL COMMENTARY: Afghan poetry frequently uses mountain imagery as a symbol of the homeland, spiritual elevation, and endurance. The Hindu Kush range — whose peaks exceed 7,000 metres — is both physical reality and national symbol for Afghans. Snow at the summit / flowers at the foot is a classic image of divine order and seasonal cycle. The phrase د خدای نظر دی [da xudāy nazar da] — 'it is under God's gaze/care' — reflects the Islamic concept of divine providence woven into everyday Pashtun speech.

content.heading: Knowledge Check — Review Before the Quiz
lines: Review these key points from Lesson 5 before attempting the quiz:
✓ Pashto adjectives agree with the noun they modify in GENDER, NUMBER, and CASE — three dimensions simultaneously.
✓ Class 1 (e.g., جوړ [joṛ], سپين [spin]): M forms unchanged except M OPl adds -و [-o]. F forms: -ه [a] DSg, -ې [e] DPl/OSg, -و [o] OPl.
✓ Class 2 (e.g., نوی [naway]): M DSg -ای [-ay], M DPl/OSg -ي [-i]. F DSg/DPl/OSg -ې [e], all OPl -و [o].
✓ Class 4 (e.g., ښايسته [xāysta], منتظر [muntazir]): ALL FORMS IDENTICAL — never changes.
✓ Class 5 (e.g., سوړ [soṛ]): M forms vary in vowel; F forms use -ه/-ه/-ه/-و pattern.
✓ Irregular: تود [tod] → تاوده [tāwda] (F); ستر [ster] → سترن [stern] (suppletive plural).
✓ Attributive = before noun. Predicative = after copula. Both follow same agreement rules.
✓ Oblique position = inside prepositional phrase. Noun AND adjective both take oblique form.
✓ Class 1 masculine oblique = same as direct (no change!). Class 2 masculine oblique -ای → -ي.
✓ Colour symbolism: سپين = purity, تور = mourning, شين = Islam/nature, سور = life/celebration.
✓ Vocabulary question: پهـ پښتو کې ... ته څه وايي؟ [pa paxto ke ... ta tse wāyi?]

content.question: What are the three grammatical dimensions Pashto adjectives must agree with?
options: Tense, aspect, and mood — matching the verb in the sentence.
Gender, number, and case — matching the noun they modify.
Person, number, and gender — matching the subject of the sentence.
Case, definiteness, and animacy — matching the noun class.
content.explanation: Pashto adjectives agree with the noun they modify in three dimensions simultaneously: (1) GENDER — masculine or feminine, (2) NUMBER — singular or plural, and (3) CASE — direct or oblique. This means a single adjective like نوی [naway] 'new' can take up to eight different surface forms (4 direct + 4 oblique cells). This is unlike English where adjectives never change form, and unlike many languages where adjectives only agree in one or two dimensions.

content.question: Which adjective class has ALL FORMS IDENTICAL — the adjective never changes regardless of gender, number, or case?
options: Class 1 — because masculine forms are always the same.
Class 2 — because the -ای [-ay] ending is invariable.
Class 4 — adjectives like ښايسته [xāysta] and منتظر [muntazir] are completely invariable.
Class 5 — because only the feminine oblique plural changes.
content.explanation: Class 4 adjectives are completely invariable — the same form is used for all genders, numbers, and cases. Examples include ښايسته [xāysta] 'beautiful', منتظر [muntazir] 'waiting', ماریض [māriz] 'sick', and خاص [xās] 'special'. Most borrowed adjectives from Arabic or Persian fall into this class. This is a welcome relief after learning the complex paradigms of Classes 1 and 2! Class 1 masculine forms are mostly unchanged, but the feminine and oblique plural forms do change.

content.question: The sentence is: پهـ سپينه ليلیه کې — 'in the white dormitory'. What form of سپين [spin] 'white' is used and why?
options: سپين [spin] — masculine direct singular, because سپين is always masculine.
سپينه [spina] — masculine direct singular, because the adjective doesn't change inside prepositional phrases.
سپينې [spine] — feminine oblique singular, because ليلیه [layliyā] is feminine and is in the oblique case as the object of پهـ...کې.
سپينو [spino] — feminine oblique plural, because prepositional phrases always take plural oblique forms.
content.explanation: پهـ سپينې ليلیه کې [pa spine layliyā ke] — 'in the white dormitory.' ليلیه [layliyā] is an F1 feminine noun. Inside the circumposition پهـ...کې, the noun takes OBLIQUE SINGULAR (OSg) case. Therefore the adjective سپين must take its F OSg form: سپين (M DSg) → سپينه (F DSg) → سپينې (F OSg). Note that for Class 1 masculine nouns, the oblique form is the same as the direct: پهـ سپين کور کې [pa spin kor ke] 'in the white house' — no change to سپين because کور is masculine and Class 1 M OSg = M DSg.

content.question: How does هوا ډيره سړه ده [hawā dera sara da] demonstrate adjective agreement?
options: هوا [hawā] is masculine, so both adjectives take masculine Class 1 forms.
هوا [hawā] is F3 feminine, so ډير [der] becomes ډيره [dera] and سوړ [soṛ] becomes سړه [sara] — both take feminine direct singular forms.
The adjectives agree with the verb ده [da] rather than the noun هوا [hawā].
There is no agreement here — ډيره and سړه are irregular uninflected intensifiers.
content.explanation: هوا [hawā] 'weather' is a feminine F3 noun. In the predicative sentence هوا ډيره سړه ده — 'The weather is very cold' — both adjectives must agree with the SUBJECT هوا in gender (feminine) and case (direct singular). ډير [der] 'very/many' is a Class 1 adjective: its F DSg form is ډيره [dera]. سوړ [soṛ] 'cold' is a Class 5 adjective: its F DSg form is سړه [sara] (the vowel shifts from [o] to [a]). Both adjectives independently agree with هوا — this is the predicate agreement rule in action.

content.question: The irregular adjective ستر [ster] 'big/great' has a suppletive plural. What does 'suppletive' mean here?
options: The plural is formed by adding a regular suffix -ان [-ān] to the singular: ستران [sterān].
The plural uses a completely different stem سترن [stern] that is historically unrelated to the singular ستر [ster].
The plural is identical to the singular — Class 4 pattern applies.
The plural is formed by doubling the final consonant: سترر [sterr].
content.explanation: 'Suppletive' means the plural form uses a completely different stem from the singular — the forms come from different historical roots. ستر [ster] (singular 'big/great') and سترن [stern] (plural 'big/great') are historically unrelated words that have merged in meaning. This is the same phenomenon as English 'go / went' (not 'goed') or 'good / better' (not 'gooder'). In Pashto, suppletive adjective plurals are rare but must be memorised individually. The oblique plural is سترنو [sterno] — built on the plural stem سترن [stern].

content.question: In the poem پهـ لوړو غرو د خدای نظر دی — what grammatical form is لوړو [loṛo] and why?
options: Masculine direct plural (M DPl) — because غر [ghar] 'mountain' is masculine and there are multiple mountains.
Masculine oblique plural (M OPl) — because غرو [gharo] is the oblique plural of غر [ghar] as the object of پهـ...کې, and the adjective لوړ must agree in case.
Feminine oblique singular (F OSg) — because the mountains are personified as feminine in the poem.
Class 4 invariable form — because all adjectives in poetry are invariable.
content.explanation: لوړو [loṛo] is the MASCULINE OBLIQUE PLURAL (M OPl) form of the Class 1 adjective لوړ [loṛ] 'high'. The phrase پهـ لوړو غرو [pa loṛo gharo] places the noun غر [ghar] 'mountain' inside the prepositional phrase پهـ...کې — making it oblique. غر [ghar] is masculine, and the plural oblique of M2 nouns takes -و [-o]: غرو [gharo]. The adjective لوړ [loṛ] must match this in all three dimensions: masculine ✓, plural ✓, oblique ✓ → M OPl form: لوړو [loṛo] (adding -و [-o] — the only change for Class 1 masculine adjectives). This single phrase is the entire oblique agreement system in miniature.
